name: Dubnium
name_origin: Named after Dubna, the Russian town that hosts the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research (JINR), one of the laboratories credited with the element's discovery.

discovery.discoverer: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research, Dubna and Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory teams

occurrence.abundance_summary: Dubnium does not occur naturally. It is a synthetic element produced in particle accelerators by fusing lighter nuclei such as 22Ne with actinide targets.
states: synthetic, produced atom-by-atom in particle accelerators

compounds.name: Dubnium pentachloride
compounds.summary: A volatile pentachloride inferred from gas-phase chromatography studies; volatility behavior aligns dubnium with group 5 elements such as niobium and tantalum.

reactivity_summary: Dubnium is expected to behave as a group 5 transition metal, chemically resembling tantalum and niobium with a dominant +5 oxidation state. Aqueous-phase studies suggest its hydrolysis and complex-formation behavior is closer to that of protactinium and niobium than to tantalum, an effect attributed to relativistic orbital contractions.
isotopes_summary: All known dubnium isotopes are radioactive. The longest-lived confirmed isotope is 268Db with a half-life on the order of 1 day; lighter isotopes such as 262Db and 263Db, with half-lives of seconds, have been used for chemical investigations.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Dubnium
Kratz, J.V. 'Aqueous Chemistry of the Heaviest Elements', Radiochim. Acta, 2011
